title: Choosing the Right Model
description: Navigate the model landscape — code, reasoning, and general purpose.

stops.title: Code Generation: Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B
stops.narrative:
For pure code generation, [Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B](node:qwen25-coder-32b) is the sweet spot. It matches GPT-4o on HumanEval (~92%) and fits at Q8_0 (~34GB) with plenty of room for its full 128K context.

The newer [Qwen3-Coder-Next](node:qwen3-coder-next) (80B MoE, 3B active) is even stronger and was specifically trained for agentic coding. It uses ~46GB and has 256K native context. If your hardware handles it well, it's the better choice for [Claude Code](node:claude-code) integration thanks to its tool calling support.

stops.title: Agentic Coding: Devstral
stops.narrative:
[Devstral Small 2](node:devstral-small-2) at 24B is purpose-built for agentic coding — plan, execute, verify loops. It scores 68% on SWE-bench Verified with an Apache 2.0 license.

Its big sibling [Devstral 2](node:devstral-2) at 123B scores 72.2% on SWE-bench but uses ~75GB at Q4_K_M, leaving limited room for context. The small version is often the better practical choice.

stops.title: Reasoning: DeepSeek R1 Distills
stops.narrative:
For architecture decisions, complex debugging, and planning, you want a reasoning model. [DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B](node:deepseek-r1-distill-70b) is the strongest that fits on 128GB — at Q5_K_M it uses ~50GB.

It produces explicit chain-of-thought in <think> blocks before answering, making its reasoning transparent and auditable.

The efficiency alternative is [DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B](node:deepseek-r1-distill-32b) at ~20GB, or [QwQ-32B](node:qwq-32b) which offers a different reasoning flavor at the same size.

stops.title: General Purpose: The 70B Tier
stops.narrative:
For general tasks that need both code ability and broad knowledge, the 70B tier is where 128GB hardware shines. [Llama 3.3 70B](node:llama-3.3-70b) matches the 405B model's quality. [Qwen 2.5 72B](node:qwen25-72b) is competitive with GPT-4 on many benchmarks.

Both fit at Q5_K_M (~50GB) or Q6_K (~58-60GB) with room for substantial context. You can't run them simultaneously with other large models, but swapping is fast on unified memory.

stops.title: The Draft Model: Phi-4
stops.narrative:
[Phi-4 14B](node:phi-4) is remarkable for its size — HumanEval ~82% in just 14B parameters. At Q8_0 it uses only ~15GB.

Use it as a fast secondary model: inline completions via [Tabby](node:tabby) or [Continue.dev](node:continue-dev), quick Q&A, or draft generation that a larger model refines. It can run alongside any other model without memory pressure.

The practical toolkit for 128GB: [Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B](node:qwen25-coder-32b) for code (34GB) + [Phi-4](node:phi-4) for quick tasks (15GB) = 49GB, leaving 79GB for context and OS.

stops.title: Where to Get Models
stops.narrative:
All models are available on [HuggingFace](node:huggingface). For [GGUF](node:gguf) files, look for uploads from [bartowski](node:bartowski) (fastest to publish, multiple quant levels) and [Unsloth](node:unsloth) (Dynamic 2.0 methodology for improved quality).

The [Ollama Library](node:ollama-library) is simpler — one-command downloads with standardized tags — but has fewer quantization options. Start with Ollama for convenience, switch to HuggingFace GGUFs when you want specific quant levels.
